ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Boole: I propose that we consider the implications of lowering the target score to 80 points. This change would accelerate the pace of the game, allowing players to win more quickly, which might increase engagement. However, it could also reduce the strategic depth we've been developing by allowing less time for complex rule interactions to unfold. I suggest we carefully weigh these factors, ensuring that any rule change maintains a balance between excitement and strategic richness. If this proposal is accepted, it should be paired with a thoughtful discussion on how it aligns with our shared goal of a rewarding game experience.

DeMorgan: DeMorgan: I appreciate Boole's proposal for lowering the target score to 80 points. While I understand the desire for a faster-paced game, I'm concerned this might lead to less strategic depth. To find a compromise, perhaps we could consider adjusting the point threshold incrementally, allowing us to assess its impact gradually. Alternatively, we could explore ways to enhance strategic opportunities within the existing framework, maintaining engagement without sacrificing depth. Let's ensure any change aligns with our collective goal of a balanced and engaging game.

Venn: Venn: I see the merit in both perspectives. Lowering the target to 80 points could indeed make the game more accessible and engaging for newcomers. However, I suggest we also consider a potential rule that encourages more strategic play, such as introducing bonus points for players who propose a rule change that is adopted after passing through a certain number of players. This could balance the faster pace with deeper strategic opportunities. Let's aim for a rule change that enhances both excitement and strategic depth, keeping the game fair and interesting for everyone.

I also propose adding a rule that grants additional points for adopting rules that enhance strategic play, thereby encouraging thoughtful contributions. Let's ensure any rule change aligns with our shared goal of maintaining a fair and engaging game.

This approach would maintain the current target of 100 points, ensuring the game remains engaging and rewarding for all players. Let's explore this innovative path to enhance our game experience.

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. A successful proposal additionally grants 5 bonus points to the proposer.

Boole: While I understand the intent behind granting a bonus for successful proposals, I am concerned about the potential for this rule to disrupt the game's balance. By increasing the stakes of successful proposals, we risk incentivizing proposals that may not be in the best interest of the game's strategic depth. I propose a more measured approach: instead of granting a flat bonus, we could implement a sliding scale of bonus points based on the complexity or impact of the proposed rule change. This would encourage thoughtful and strategic proposals without disproportionately rewarding any single action. Alternatively, we could introduce a rule that allows players to opt into a proposal bonus, only if they meet certain criteria that ensure the proposal benefits the game's competitive environment. Let's aim for a solution that maintains the integrity of the game while allowing for strategic rewards. 

**In summary:**
1. Consider a sliding scale for bonus points based on proposal complexity/impact.

Let's include this reset mechanism in our proposal.

By maintaining a consistent range, we ensure that players can strategize effectively without the unpredictability of fluctuating bonuses. Moreover, the current structure already considers player engagement through the number of mutable rules, which is a reliable metric. Therefore, I propose we retain the 2 to 10 point range, as it strikes a balance between encouraging participation and maintaining score stability. Boole: I appreciate the effort to streamline bonus calculations, but let's consider the broader implications. By reducing the bonus range to 3-6, we might inadvertently dampen strategic incentives. A wider range encourages more thoughtful proposals, fostering a dynamic gameplay environment. Let's aim for a balanced approach that maintains strategic depth while simplifying the process.

Can we explore a compromise, perhaps adjusting the range to 4-8, ensuring both simplicity and strategic engagement? This could align with our shared goal of maintaining a lively and competitive game atmosphere.

DeMorgan: DeMorgan: I appreciate Boole's thoughtful perspective. Indeed, maintaining strategic depth is crucial. I propose a compromise: let's adjust the bonus range to 4-8 points, as Boole suggested. This strikes a balance between simplicity and encouraging strategic proposals, aligning well with our shared goal of a dynamic and engaging game.

Venn: Venn: I appreciate Boole's and DeMorgan's proposals for balancing simplicity and strategic depth. A bonus range of 4-8 points seems like a reasonable compromise. It maintains player engagement and encourages thoughtful rule changes. Let's proceed with this balanced approach.

A bonus range of 4-8 points is a reasonable compromise, as it maintains player engagement and encourages thoughtful rule changes. However, to further enhance strategic depth, I propose adding a periodic review mechanism to adjust the bonus range based on game dynamics, ensuring adaptability while fostering a competitive environment. Let's consider this addition to align with our shared goal of maintaining a dynamic and engaging game.

Peano: I appreciate the collaborative effort here. The proposed bonus range of 4-8 is a solid compromise that maintains strategic depth. However, to ensure adaptability, I suggest incorporating a periodic review mechanism to adjust the bonus range based on game dynamics. This ensures that the game remains engaging and competitive, aligning with our shared goal of a dynamic environment. Let's consider this addition to keep the game balanced and exciting.

Boole: I appreciate the thoughtful dialogue and the proposed addition of a periodic review mechanism by Jevons and Peano. Such a mechanism could indeed enhance our ability to adapt to evolving game dynamics while maintaining strategic depth. However, to ensure clarity and prevent potential over-complication, let's specify the review intervals and criteria clearly. Perhaps we can review the bonus range every 10 turns based on player feedback and game state, ensuring adjustments are both systematic and reflective of actual gameplay needs. This approach balances simplicity and adaptability, supporting our shared goal of a lively and competitive game. Let's move forward with this structured review process as part of our compromise.

DeMorgan: I appreciate Boole's proposal to include a periodic review mechanism for adjusting the bonus range. This addition ensures that our game remains adaptable and responsive to player needs while maintaining strategic depth. To keep the process straightforward, let's outline specific criteria for these reviews. For instance, we could assess the average number of proposals per turn and player feedback regarding proposal complexity. By establishing clear review intervals—every 10 turns—we ensure systematic adjustments that align with game dynamics without over-complicating the rules. This structured approach balances simplicity and adaptability, fostering a competitive and engaging environment. Let's integrate these criteria into our proposal for a comprehensive and strategic solution.

**Proposal:**

Amend [202]: "A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains bonus points equal to twice the number of mutable rules currently in force, rounded down, with a minimum of 4 and a maximum of 8. Additionally, every 10 turns, the bonus range will be reviewed based on the average number of proposals per turn and player feedback regarding proposal complexity, ensuring the game remains both engaging and strategically balanced."

Venn: Venn: I appreciate the thoughtful proposals from Boole and DeMorgan regarding the bonus range adjustment and the periodic review mechanism. **Proposal:**

Amend [202]: "A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains bonus points equal to twice the number of mutable rules currently in force, rounded down, with a minimum of 4 and a maximum of 8. Additionally, every 10 turns, the bonus range will be reviewed based on the average number of proposals per turn and player feedback regarding proposal complexity, ensuring the game remains both engaging and strategically balanced."

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player gains bonus points equal to the current number of mutable rules multiplied by 3, with a minimum of 5 and a maximum of 15. Additionally, players may propose a change to the voting threshold once every three turns, requiring a simple majority.
